draft.introduction: The parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25–37) is Jesus’ answer to a lawyer’s question about inheriting eternal life. It redefines “neighbor” across ethnic and religious boundaries.

translation.text: And behold, a lawyer stood up to put him to the test, saying, ‘Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?’ He said to him, ‘What is written in the Law? How do you read it?’
translation.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ νομικός τις ἀνέστη ἐκπειράζων αὐτὸν λέγων · διδάσκαλε τί ποιήσας ζωὴν αἰώνιον κληρονομήσω

translation.text: But a Samaritan, as he journeyed, came to where he was, and when he saw him, he had compassion. He went to him and bound up his wounds. ‘Which of these three proved to be a neighbor?’ He said, ‘The one who showed him mercy.’ Jesus said, ‘Go and do likewise.’
translation.original_text: Σαμαρείτης δέ τις ὁδεύων ἦλθεν κατ’ αὐτὸν καὶ ἰδὼν ἐσπλαγχνίσθη

translator_notes.note: The choice of a Samaritan as moral exemplar would have been shocking to a Jewish audience. Samaritans were considered heretical outsiders, making this parable a deliberate subversion of ethnic-religious categories.
